html:
BIT Computer (KRX:032850)

BIT Computer is not the zero-EV cash box described in some stale writeups, and it is not a clean SaaS compounder either. The better thesis is more interesting: a tiny, ignored Korean EMR/HIS vendor with real switching costs, a growing cloud mix, a net-cash balance sheet, active buybacks, insider buying, and a new export contract that can make 2026 the first year in a while where the market has to underwrite forward earnings instead of stale flat revenue.

At ₩4,825 on May 18, 2026, BIT has an approximately ₩80 billion market cap (StockAnalysis, Butler). Year-end 2025 cash and short-term financial assets were about ₩29.2 billion against only ₩0.3 billion of debt, with tangible book value near ₩69 billion (StockAnalysis). That matters, but the stock should not be bought merely because it has cash. The actual forward setup is that the operating business is being valued at roughly 6.5-8x forward free cash flow while Q1 2026 already showed growth returning.

The core perception error is that investors still treat BIT as a sleepy KOSDAQ medical IT contractor that only moves on telemedicine headlines. The operating company is better described as a Korean healthcare workflow platform. Its product set includes hospital information systems, EMR, OCS, clinic EMR, cloud subscription EMR, telemedicine platform BaroDoctor, and IT training assets. FnGuide’s company summary notes bitnixHIS for larger hospitals, CLEMR cloud subscription EMR for midsize hospitals, Bit Plus for clinics, and AI functionality being added to the workflow stack (FnGuide). A 2024 KIRS report described BIT as No.1 in the secondary hospital EMR market and No.2 in clinics, with medical information systems nearly 80% of sales and cloud-packaged EMR carrying materially better gross margin than build-type projects (KIRS/Paxnet PDF).

The 2025 numbers looked dull on the surface: revenue ₩36.0 billion, operating profit about ₩5.9-6.2 billion, net income ₩7.3 billion, and free cash flow ₩6.6 billion. But Q1 2026 was the first clean signal that the pause may be ending: revenue ₩9.6 billion, operating profit ₩1.8 billion, net income ₩1.8 billion, up 12% / 19% / 14% YoY, with the highest operating profit in the last five reported quarters (Awakeplus). Annualizing Q1 is too simple, but it does suggest a 2026 run-rate closer to ₩7.5-8.5 billion of operating profit before any stronger cloud mix benefit.

The March 2026 Cambodia Ministry of Health contract is the overlooked forward earnings lever. BIT signed a ₩6.3 billion HIS implementation contract, equal to 16.97% of prior-year sales, running from March 2026 to February 2027 (Awakeplus). Even if this is lower-margin implementation revenue, it changes the 2026 revenue shape. A business that was stuck around ₩34-37 billion of annual sales can plausibly move into the ₩42-45 billion range this year. If operating margins stay around 18-19%, rather than the 16.3% reported in 2025, net income can move toward ₩8.0-9.0 billion and FCF toward ₩7.5-8.5 billion.

That makes today’s valuation too low. At ₩4,825, the stock is about 10-11x trailing earnings, roughly 9x my 2026 base-case earnings, and about 12x trailing FCF. Adjusting for cash and short-term financial assets, the operating enterprise value is around ₩51 billion, which is about 6-7x forward FCF. This is not a fantasy multiple case. A profitable, net-cash, healthcare software company with 18% operating margins, 20% net margins, cloud mix growth, and a material 2026 revenue catalyst should not trade at a single-digit forward ex-cash multiple unless the market believes the business is structurally ex-growth or the cash will never benefit minority holders.

The capital return signal is improving. BIT repurchased ₩2.4 billion of stock in 2025, completed another 192,598-share, ₩1.0 billion buyback in April 2026, and on May 14 announced a further 208,560-share, ₩1.0 billion direct-market buyback through August 14, 2026 (Awakeplus). That is small in absolute money, but meaningful against an ₩80 billion market cap and tiny institutional float. Add the ₩66 dividend, and the shareholder yield is quietly respectable. The missing piece is cancellation. Buybacks held forever as treasury shares are less valuable than retirements, so the thesis should give management credit for price support and capital discipline, but not full credit for a shrinking share count until cancellations actually happen.

Insiders are also acting like the stock is cheap. The founder-chairman bought stock in April around ₩5,267, the CEO bought around ₩5,410, directors added small amounts, and one director filed a new 204,947-share position around ₩5,124 in May (Butler). None of that proves upside, but it matters in a Korean microcap where governance and capital allocation are the central discount.

The biggest current overhang is not earnings. It is regulatory pressure around Silson24, Korea’s paperless indemnity insurance claim service. Seoul Economic Daily reported that the government is pressuring EMR firms that have not joined the service, naming Bit Computer as a clinic-level EMR provider with about 3,600 clinic clients and roughly 9.7% share; authorities also raised the possibility of Fair Trade Commission scrutiny (Seoul Economic Daily). This is real risk: BIT may have to accept lower integration economics, spend engineering resources, or lose political goodwill. But it is also a proof point that EMR vendors control an essential tollgate. The government is not pressuring irrelevant software vendors. It is pressuring the companies embedded deeply enough in clinic workflow to determine whether a national insurance digitization project works.

The cleanest variant view is this: BIT is priced as a no-growth local IT contractor just as 2026 revenue, margins, and shareholder returns are all turning in the right direction. The market has no sell-side consensus for the stock, almost no fund ownership, and mostly sees a low-liquidity retail tape. If Q2 and Q3 show Cambodia revenue recognition, stable domestic EMR margins, and continued buybacks, the market can move from “dead medical IT value trap” to “profitable Korean healthcare software platform at 9x forward earnings.” That rerating does not require a heroic SaaS multiple.

Downside protection is solid but not magical. Tangible book is around ₩4,500 per share, net cash and short-term financial assets are roughly ₩1,850 per share, and the company is profitable. But Korean small-cap value traps can trade below book for years, especially when cash is not aggressively returned. The real bear case is regulatory friction, low-margin government or export projects, no treasury retirement, and flat domestic EMR growth. In that case, the stock can trade at 0.8x tangible book even while remaining financially sound.

The bull case comes from three things happening together: cloud EMR mix lifts structural margins, Cambodia becomes a reference export rather than a one-off, and management converts treasury stock from symbolic buybacks into actual cancellation or a larger return program. In that scenario, BIT can earn ₩10 billion after tax within two years, carry surplus financial assets, and deserve 14-16x earnings or a cash-adjusted software multiple. That does not require AI data monetization or an acquisition, though either would add optionality.

Targets from ₩4,825 current price:

Bear: ₩3,800, down 21%. Regulatory pressure compresses economics, Cambodia is low-margin, earnings stay around ₩6.5-7.0 billion, and the market values the company below tangible book because buybacks are not retired.

Base: ₩7,200, up 49%. 2026 revenue reaches ₩42-44 billion, net income reaches about ₩8.3 billion, FCF approaches ₩8 billion, and the market applies a still-modest 13x forward EPS or about 10x cash-adjusted FCF.

Bull: ₩11,500, up 138%. Cloud mix and exports push net income toward ₩10 billion, buybacks continue and begin to reduce economic share count, and the market values BIT as a profitable healthcare software platform rather than a generic KOSDAQ IT contractor.

The stock is not a perfect compounder, and it is not liquid enough for large capital. But for a small position, the forward setup is attractive: limited balance-sheet downside, improving earnings evidence, active capital return, insider alignment, regulatory proof of strategic relevance, and a valuation that still prices the business as if nothing is changing.
